content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Half the reason people never try a new AI tool is the wall in front of it: create an account, confirm your email, maybe add a card “just to verify.” For a five-minute task, that is more setup than the task.
So here are seven AI tools you can actually open and use right now, no account, no email. I tried each one the lazy way, the way a normal person would, to see how far you get before something asks you to sign up.
1. GetAskAI, a full AI chat with no account
GetAskAI is the simplest version of the pitch: open the page, type, get an answer. No login, no app, no message counter. In a long session I never hit a limit.
What makes it more than a toy: it answers in whatever language you write in, it searches the web when you ask about something current and shows the sources instead of guessing, and it reads images and PDFs. It is not chasing the “smartest model” crown, it leans on solid free models, but for “just answer me, no setup,” it is the one I keep open.
Use it for: everyday questions, quick research with sources, summarising a PDF, anything you would rather not tie to an account.
2. DuckDuckGo AI Chat, the private one
At duck.ai you get a stripped-down chat with a choice of a few smaller models, no account and nothing logged to a profile. It forgets the conversation on purpose. It is deliberately lean, so do not expect long, deep reasoning, but for a quick private question it is exactly enough.
Use it for: anything personal you would rather not attach to your name.
3. Perplexity, answers with sources
Perplexity is an answer engine wearing a chatbot’s clothes. Ask a question and it searches, answers, and links its sources, with no login needed for the basics. The deeper “Pro” mode is counted, but the free version covers most “what is the deal with X” moments.
Use it for: fact-checking and quick research where you want to see where the answer came from.
4. ChatGPT, yes, without an account
You can now use ChatGPT without logging in. It is the default for a reason, it is very good, but the free-no-login version rations the best model and slows you down if you lean on it. Fine for a few sharp questions, not for a marathon.
Use it for: a couple of high-quality answers when you want the name-brand model.
5. Hugging Face Spaces, thousands of demos to try
Hugging Face Spaces is a giant gallery of AI demos people have published: image tools, transcription, chat, niche models, and you can try a huge number of them in the browser without signing up. Quality is all over the map because anyone can post one, but it is the fastest way to try a specific model without committing to anything.
Use it for: trying one specific or unusual model once, without installing or registering.
6. LMArena, compare top models side by side
LMArena, the “Chatbot Arena,” lets you ask a question and see two anonymous top models answer side by side, then you pick the better one, no account needed to play with it. It is the easiest way to feel the difference between the frontier models without paying for all of them.
Use it for: settling “which model is actually better for my kind of question.”
7. Craiyon, free image generation, no signup
Not everything is chat. Craiyon makes images from a text prompt with no account at all. It will not beat the paid image models on polish, but for a quick throwaway illustration or a laugh, you type and it draws, nothing to sign.
Use it for: a fast, disposable image when you do not want to burn a paid credit.
The pattern
Notice the trade. The tools that ask for nothing tend to be either lean on purpose (DuckDuckGo, Craiyon), a gallery of other people’s work (Hugging Face), or funded another way so they can stay open (GetAskAI). The tools with the smartest models are also the quickest to put a login or a limit in front of you, because that is how they manage cost.
For most quick tasks you do not need the smartest model. You need the one you can open right now. Bookmark two or three of these and you will reach for an account far less often than you think.
Start with GetAskAI for chat and Perplexity for anything you need sourced, add DuckDuckGo for private questions, and you have covered most of what people open ChatGPT for, without the sign-up.
